1: The Queen of Sheba’s pilgrimage to Solomon with “difficult questions” (ḥîḏôṯ, riddles/hard sayings) is the paradigmatic Gentile encounter with divine wisdom in the Hebrew Bible. Jesus cites her as the eschatological witness against his generation: “The queen of the South will rise at the judgment with the people of this generation and condemn them, because she came from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon’s wisdom — and look, something greater than Solomon is here” (Matthew 12:42; Luke 11:31). Her journey from the ends of the earth prefigures the nations’ eschatological ingathering to the one who is wisdom incarnate (1 Corinthians 1:30; Colossians 2:3).
2: Solomon answered all her questions — “nothing was hidden from Solomon that he could not explain to her.” The comprehensiveness of Solomon’s wisdom-answers is a shadow of the one in whom “all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden” (Colossians 2:3). When the disciples ask Jesus about the destruction of the temple (Matthew 24:3), about the greatest commandment (Matthew 22:36), about the resurrection (Matthew 22:23-32), about taxes (Matthew 22:15-22) — no question exceeds his capacity to answer; every question becomes an occasion for revelation beyond what the questioner anticipated.
3: The queen observes not only Solomon’s wisdom but its material expressions — the palace, the organizational systems, the service. In Chronicles, wisdom always has concrete institutional form: the right organization of temple service, royal administration, and architectural beauty. Christ’s wisdom similarly has concrete form in the church’s ordered life (1 Cor 12-14; Ephesians 4:11-16), the community that embodies his wisdom before the powers (Ephesians 3:10).
4: The full vision of Solomonic glory — table, officials, attendants, cupbearers, the ascent to the temple — overwhelms the queen; “there was no more breath in her” (the Hebrew idiom for being overcome). The complete integration of feasting, service, and worship in Solomon’s court is the type of the eschatological feast where the Lord serves his own at table (Luke 12:37: “he will dress himself to serve, will have them recline at the table and will come and wait on them”) — the king who is also servant, the host who is also butler.
5: The queen confesses that the report was true and that she did not believe it until she saw it. The pattern of hearing-then-seeing, of testimony preceding experience, runs through Scripture: the Samaritans say to the woman, “We no longer believe just because of what you said; now we have heard for ourselves” (John 4:42). The queen’s journey from report to presence is the structure of faith — and of the gospel that moves from testimony to encounter.
6: “Not even half of your great wisdom was reported to me.” The excess of reality over testimony is a structural principle of Christ’s glory: “Eye has not seen, ear has not heard, and the heart of man has not imagined, what God has prepared for those who love him” (1 Corinthians 2:9). Every account of Christ’s glory in Scripture falls short of the reality (Revelation 1:17: John falls as though dead at the sight of the risen Christ); the queen’s confession that she underestimated Solomon foreshadows every believer’s experience of the one greater than Solomon.
7: “How fortunate are your men, and how blessed are these servants of yours who stand before you continually.” The beatitude for those who stand in Solomon’s presence reflects the beatitude of worship — those who dwell in YHWH’s presence are blessed (Psalms 84:4: “How blessed are those who dwell in your house, who are ever praising you”). Christ pronounces his own version of this beatitude on those who witness what prophets and kings longed to see: “Blessed are the eyes that see what you see” (Luke 10:23). The servants standing before the king is the prototype of the church standing in the presence of the greater Solomon.
8: The queen’s doxology: “Blessed be the LORD your God, who has delighted in you and placed you on his throne as king for the LORD your God.” A Gentile queen pronounces a blessing on YHWH through the king who represents him — and identifies the king’s throne as YHWH’s throne. This is the Chronicler’s royal theology made explicit by a pagan: Solomon sits on “the throne of YHWH” (1 Chronicles 29:23) as YHWH’s representative. The queen unwittingly confesses what the NT states directly: the Father has placed the Son on the throne because of his delight in him (Matthew 3:17: “This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased”; Hebrews 1:8: “Your throne, O God, is forever”).
9: The queen’s gifts: 120 talents of gold, enormous spices, precious stones. Gentile royalty bringing gold and spices to the king who sits on YHWH’s throne is the template the Magi fulfill (Matthew 2:11: “they offered him gifts of gold, frankincense, and myrrh”). The Magi’s gifts are not arbitrary; they follow the pattern of the Queen of Sheba’s tribute and the royal Psalm’s vision (Psalms 72:10-11: “The kings of Tarshish and the coasts will render tribute; the kings of Sheba and Seba will bring gifts; all kings will bow down to him”). The queen’s gold is the type; the Magi’s gold is the partial fulfillment; the ingathering of nations to Christ is the completion.
10: The servants of Huram and Solomon bring gold from Ophir along with algum wood and precious stones — a joint Tyrian-Israelite commercial operation bringing building materials and luxury goods. The collaboration of Gentile craft and Israelite worship direction to build YHWH’s house prefigures the grafting of the Gentiles into the one olive tree (Romans 11:17-24), the joint participation of Jew and Gentile in building the spiritual house (Ephesians 2:19-22: “you are being built together into a dwelling place of God in the Spirit”).
11: The algum wood is used for steps in the temple and for harps and lyres — worship infrastructure. The instruments of praise are built from the same wood as the temple’s structure; music is architecturally integrated into the house. In the new creation, the new song (Revelation 5:9; 2 Chronicles 14:3) is similarly inseparable from the temple — the whole city is the temple and the whole temple resounds with the song of the redeemed from every nation.
12: Solomon gives the queen “everything she desired and asked for, over and above what she had brought to the king.” The divine economy of the covenant is not equivalent exchange but superabundance: what the queen brings is returned with excess. This is the pattern of grace: “With the measure you use, it will be measured to you — and more will be added to you” (Mark 4:24); “He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also along with him graciously give us all things?” (Romans 8:32). The return exceeds the gift.
13: Solomon’s annual gold income: 666 talents. The number is not symbolic in Chronicles; it records Solomon’s actual revenue. But the accumulation of gold beyond the limit set in Deuteronomy 17:17 (“he must not accumulate large amounts of silver and gold for himself”) marks Solomon’s drift from the Torah’s vision of kingship. The one who accumulates wealth without limit becomes a king for himself rather than for the LORD. Revelation’s use of 666 for the beast (Revelation 13:18) may draw on this association of unlimited wealth-accumulation with idolatrous power. Christ explicitly refuses this path: “The Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head” (Matthew 8:20).
14: Additional income from merchants, traders, and tributary kings — the economic network of Solomon’s empire. The universal tribute paid to Solomon (all the kings of Arabia, regional governors) is the earthly shadow of the eschatological tribute to the king of kings: “The nations will walk by its light, and the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it” (Revelation 21:24). But Solomon’s tribute is coercive taxation; Christ’s tribute is the free gift of redeemed nations laying down their glory before the Lamb.
15: Two hundred large shields of hammered gold — display weapons never intended for battlefield use, hung in the House of the Forest of Lebanon. They represent Solomon’s wealth-as-glory: gold armor signals that he has transcended the need for iron. But this glory is vulnerable, as 2 Chronicles 12 demonstrates when Shishak takes them and Rehoboam replaces them with bronze. Christ’s glory is not the kind that can be stripped: “the glory that I had with you before the world existed” (John 17:5) is not material display but the eternal love between Father and Son.
16: Three hundred smaller shields of gold hung in the House of the Forest. The armory of gold shields is the visible statement of a king so secure that his weapons need never leave their display case. The contrast with Rehoboam’s bronze shields (2 Chronicles 12:10) maps the dynasty’s trajectory: from gold to bronze is a declension from Solomonic glory toward judgment. Only in Christ is the Solomonic glory not only restored but surpassed — the true king does not replace gold with bronze but gives himself as the treasure that rust cannot touch (Matthew 6:19-21).
17: The great ivory throne overlaid with pure gold — the most elaborate throne description in the Hebrew Bible. Ivory (elephant tusk) and gold together signal maximum luxury and prestige. Solomon’s throne is the earthly manifestation of the divine throne; 1 Chronicles 29:23 calls it “the throne of YHWH.” The elaborateness of the throne points forward to the divine throne in Revelation: “The one seated on it had the appearance of jasper and carnelian; a rainbow that looked like an emerald surrounded the throne” (Revelation 4:3). Solomon’s throne is built with human materials; the divine throne exceeds all human construction.
18: Six steps up to the throne with a gold footstool — the king ascends six steps to reach his seat, with lions at each level. The footstool of the king is a significant royal image; YHWH himself declares: “Heaven is my throne and the earth is my footstool” (Isaiah 66:1; Acts 7:49). Psalms 110:1 — the most-quoted OT verse in the NT — declares: “The LORD says to my Lord: Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies your footstool.” Solomon’s footstool is the earthly symbol of the cosmic sovereignty that the Son exercises from the Father’s right hand.
19: Twelve lions on the six steps — one at each side of each step. The number twelve and the lion imagery together are Christological coordinates: the tribe of Judah is the lion (Genesis 49:9; Revelation 5:5: “the Lion of the tribe of Judah has triumphed”). Twelve lions flanking Solomon’s ascent to the throne prefigure the twelve apostles who will “sit on twelve thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel” (Matthew 19:28) when the true Davidic king assumes his throne. “Nothing like it existed in any other kingdom” — the throne of the greater Solomon will likewise be incomparable (Revelation 21:22-23).
20: All of Solomon’s drinking vessels were gold — nothing of silver because silver “was not considered valuable in Solomon’s days.” The banquet table of pure gold is the type of the eschatological feast: “I will not drink again of the fruit of the vine until the kingdom of God comes” (Luke 22:18). The wedding supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9) will be the feast for which Solomon’s golden banquet hall was a distant preparation — a feast not of Solomon’s accumulated wealth but of Christ’s self-giving.
21: The Tarshish fleet returning every three years with gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks — exotic tribute from the far west. The three-year rhythm and the catalogue of marvels (the rare animals especially) communicate that Solomon’s reach extends to the ends of the earth. Isaiah’s eschatological vision uses the same Tarshish imagery: “For the coastlands will look for me, and the ships of Tarshish first, to bring your sons from far away” (Isaiah 60:9). The ships that bring tribute to Solomon become the ships that bring the children of God home in the eschatological ingathering.
22: “King Solomon surpassed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom.” The superlative of Solomon’s reign is the Chronicler’s signal that this is a typological peak — the closest the Davidic monarchy came to the divine ideal. Yet even this superlative falls short: Christ is not merely the wisest king but wisdom itself (1 Corinthians 1:24, 30), not merely the richest king but the one through whom “all things were created” (Colossians 1:16). The superlative of Solomon points beyond itself to the one about whom “His name will be called Wonderful Counselor” (Isaiah 9:6).
23: “All the kings of the earth sought audiences with Solomon to hear the wisdom God had placed in his heart.” The universal pilgrimage of kings to wisdom — seeking Solomon — is the type of the universal mission of the church: rather than kings coming to Jerusalem, the disciples go to all nations (Matthew 28:19), bringing wisdom that is greater than Solomon’s. The direction reverses: instead of the world coming to Israel’s capital, Israel’s descendant sends his people to the world.
24: Annual tribute: silver, gold, clothing, myrrh, spices, horses, mules. Every year from every king — a perpetual tribute stream. The eschatological vision of Isaiah 60:5-6 echoes this: “The wealth on the seas will be brought to you, to you the riches of the nations will come; caravans of camels will cover your land, young camels of Midian and Ephah... they will come bearing gold and incense and proclaiming the praise of the LORD.” What kings brought to Solomon in silver and gold, the nations will bring to Christ in praise and worship.
25: Solomon’s horses: four thousand stalls, twelve thousand horsemen. The Chronicler has already noted that Solomon’s horses came from Egypt (2 Chronicles 1:16-17). Deuteronomy 17:16 explicitly warns the king: “he must not acquire many horses for himself or make the people return to Egypt to get more.” Solomon’s military infrastructure thus represents a slow drift from the Torah’s ideal of a king who trusts YHWH rather than military power. Christ specifically refuses the militarized messianism that horses symbolize (Revelation 6:2; Zechariah 9:9: the king “riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey” — the deliberate anti-war animal).
26: Solomon’s dominion “over all the kings from the Euphrates River to the land of the Philistines and down to the border of Egypt” — the territorial fulfillment of the Abrahamic covenant (Genesis 15:18: “from the river of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates”). This is the only moment in Israel’s history when the Abrahamic land promise finds its maximum territorial expression. Christ’s dominion exceeds even this: “Ask of me, and I will make the nations your heritage, and the ends of the earth your possession” (Psalms 2:8) — the scope is not one region of the ancient Near East but the entire creation.
27: “The king made silver as common in Jerusalem as stone.” The abundance of Solomon’s reign — where precious materials become ordinary — is the type of the new creation’s abundance: “The city was pure gold, like clear glass” (Revelation 21:18); “the street of the city was pure gold” (Revelation 21:21). In the new Jerusalem, what was previously precious becomes the pavement — the economy of glory inverts all normal scarcity. Solomon glimpses this abundance but cannot sustain it; the new creation achieves it permanently.
28: Horses brought from Egypt and all other lands — the international horse trade feeding Solomon’s military expansion, explicitly the Egypt-connection Deuteronomy 17:16 prohibits. The quiet accumulation of what was forbidden is the Chronicler’s explanation for why Solomon’s reign cannot be permanent. Christ, by contrast, builds his kingdom on the one thing that cannot be taken away — his own sacrificial obedience — rather than on the accumulation of prohibited power.
29: Three prophetic source citations: Nathan the prophet, the prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite, the visions of Iddo the seer. Solomon’s acts are preserved in prophetic witness, not merely royal annals. The Chronicler consistently grounds history in the prophetic word, not in political record-keeping alone — reflecting the biblical conviction that the prophets are the true historians, reading events in light of YHWH’s word. Christ is the word to whom all the prophets bear witness (John 5:39; Luke 24:27).
30: Forty years over all Israel in Jerusalem — the typologically resonant number of full-generation completeness (forty years in the wilderness; forty days of Jesus’ testing in Matthew 4:1-2). Solomon’s forty-year reign represents a complete era: the golden age runs its full course and ends. Every human golden age ends; the reign of Christ alone has no end (Luke 1:33: “of his kingdom there will be no end”; Hebrews 1:8: “Your throne, O God, is forever and ever”).
31: Solomon “rested with his ancestors and was buried in the city of David his father.” The regnal burial formula marks the end of the golden age. Every great king dies; the dynasty continues but the person does not. The Chronicler’s entire narrative leads to the king who does not merely “rest with his ancestors” but rises from death: “For David, after he had served God’s purpose in his own generation, fell asleep and was buried with his fathers... but the one whom God raised from the dead did not see decay” (Acts 13:36-37). Solomon’s burial in the city of David is the type that the empty tomb reverses.
